figures.caption: Fig. 3.1 Log condition numbers κ(Mn) for the scalar case of the delay matrix Mn as in (3.1) with varying lags n, along with the bound as in Proposition 3.2, are plotted against the weight parameter ω. (a) For ω away from |ω| ≈ 1, κ(Mn) is of relatively low magnitude, indicating Mn is a stable embedding. For |ω| near 1, Mn becomes ill-conditioned. (b) For |ω| = 1, the bound on κ(Mn) increases linearly on the number of lags n; increasing n makes Mn an unstable embedding.

Fig. 3.2 Log generalized determinants S(Mn) for the scalar case of delay matrices Mn as in (3.1) with varying lags n, along with the bound for |ω| < 1 as in Proposition 3.4, are plotted against the weight parameter ω. (a) For ω /∈ {ω : |ω| < 1}, S(Mn) becomes unbounded in an exponential fashion, so |ω| > 1 produces an unstable delay matrix Mn. (b) While for most ω ∈ (−1, 1), S(Mn) is near 1, indicating Mn preserves “volume" and is thus a relatively stable embedding, as |ω| → 1−, S(Mn) → ∞ and the embedding destabilizes.

Fig. 3.3 The log of the condition number of Mn,m, κ(Mn,m), for Hermitian W ∈ C2×2 is shown for varying choices of λ1(W) and λ2(W) in cases where the number of lags is (a) n = 2, (b) n = 8, and (c) n = 32. Mn,m is near-singular when |λ1(W)| or |λ2(W)| are close to 1, and its ill-conditioning increases as n increases. Notably, κ(Mn,m) is small within the unit square and away from eigenvalues of magnitude 1. (d) Noting that the structure of the plot in each direction closely resembles the scalar case 3.1, it follows that the m-dimensional case for W is the graph crossed with itself m times.

Fig. 3.4 The log of S(Mn,m) for Hermitian W ∈ C2×2 is shown for varying λ1(W) and λ2(W) and for lags (a) n = 2, (b) n = 8, and (c) n = 32. The order of magnitude of S(Mn,m) scales drastically as n increases, especially when both |λ1| > 1 and |λ2| > 1, as well as with increasing n. When |λ1| > 1 and |λ2| < 1, or vice versa, S(Mn,m) is relatively well-behaved, and the stablest region is when |λ1| < 1 and |λ2| < 1, the unit square. (d) The plot structure in both directions resembles the scalar case 3.2, so, for m-dimensional W, the graph is the scalar case crossed with itself m times.

Fig. 3.5 (a) The admissible spectrum of W lies within the blue region ranging from the dark blue lines σmin(W) and σmax(W). The area shaded in gray, which is admissible in the scalar and block Hermitian cases, represents the potential area of improved coverage if a tighter bound on the block diagonal dominance condition is even possible. The red line provides an example range from σmin(W) to σmax(W) and the toroidal section of the unit circle it defines in which the spectrum of W can reside for Mn to be full row rank and thus an embedding. (b) The associated area of the torus defined by the admissible singular value range relative to the area of the unit circle. (c) The admissible singular value range of W extended beyond σmin(W) ∈ [0, 1] to [0, 2], with the weaker result (3.24) in red and the stronger results (3.25) and (3.26) in blue. The “pinch" point where σmax(W), σmin(W) → 1 is when W is unitary.

Fig. 3.6 Log condition numbers κ(Mn,m), where the Mn,m have general W, are plotted against a continuum of choices of bounds on the maximal singular value of W, σmax(W), as well as for various choices of sizing m for W. The sub-figures depict the results for (a) n = 4, (b) n = 8, and (c) n = 16 lags. Increasing m increases the ill-conditioning of Mn,m. Note the difference in colorbar scaling.

Fig. 3.7 Log values of the generalized determinant S(Mn,m), for Mn,m with arbitrary W, are plotted against varying choices of σmax(W) and for different sizes m of W, with the sub-figures showing: (a) n = 4, (b) n = 8, and (c) n = 16 delays. Note the slight difference colorbar scaling.

Fig. 3.8 (a) shows in black the bound on κ(Mn,m) from Proposition 3.23 that is independent of m and n, along with κ(Mn,m) for various numerically-simulated Mn,m with W of varying dimensions m and lags n, plotted against σmax(W). While the bound is unbounded on a log scale as σmax(W) → 1, all the numerically-simulated results grow roughly linearly in the log-scale. Increasing m reduces κ(Mn,m) for a fixed σmax(W), while increasing n increases κ(Mn,m). (b) depicts a comparison of the singular value distributions of Mn,m for various classes of W. The distribution for the random W, which are almost always full rank, roughly approaches the distribution for when W = 0, and the distribution for unitary W is similar to that of identity W, as both types of W have unit-magnitude eigenvalues. When W = 0 or W is random, the spectrum of Mn,m approaches unity, in which case Mn,m behaves like a unitary transformation from Ψ(hk) into Φxk−1 , meaning it is almost an isometric embedding.
